    Compiling AMBER for modern GPU architecture

    The dread of every computational chemist is compiling your scientific software. If you rely on campus or government clusters, you can probably rely on real sysadmins. However, when you are running your own system, you have to roll up your sleeves and do the dirty work yourself.

    The compilation of the AMBER source code is rather straightforward (see the manual):

    1. make sure to install necessary dependencies
    2. modify the run_cmake to point to the correct install location and include the necessary cmake flags (e.g. MPI, CUDA)
    3. run $ ./run_cmake
    4. run $ make install or $ make install -jX where X is the number of parallel processes and you are done!

    For v2 of the cluster, I bought an RTX Pro 4000 card. The RTX Pro 4000 has a brandspanking new Blackwell architecture. Several issues came up after installation of the card:

    1. Black screen on boot. It displayed the startup sequence from motherboard and rendered the BIOS so it had to be a Linux specific issue and not hardware related. I found this post on Reddit which was the exact same issue! The solution? Open source drivers was all it took to correct this error.
    2. When trying the RTX Pro 4000 for AMBER, I encountered an obscure error I had never seen before: cudaMemcpyToSymbol: SetSim copy to cSim failed no kernel image is available for execution on the device. AMBER worked perfectly on the two RTX 4000 ADA cards (older architecture). I did not have to find long for a solution as it appeared quite easily when searching for this particular error in Google. As explained in this link, you need to modify some of the config files. Just for the sake of completeness, I only had 1 of those files in the src present (amber24_src/cmake/CudaConfig.cmake) and the entry on line 17 of that particular file was set(SM120FLAGS -gencode arch=compute_120,code=sm_120) simply changing this line to set(SM120FLAGS -gencode arch=compute_120,code="compute_120,sm_120") was not enough. Lines 19 and 21 had similar content but 100 and 90 instead of 120, respectively. I modified those two lines in the same way and now pmemd.cuda DID startup on the RTX Pro 4000. Great success!

    As you can see, doing your own sysadmin is often searching what you do not know and rationalizing whether the info you find makes sense or not. Anyhow, I wanted to quickly jot this down as an example of the ‘‘sysadmin’’ work I do.

    See you next time!

    Updated: March 11, 2026
